original_abstract: Electrocatalysis expands the ability to generate industrially relevant chemicals locally and on-demand with intermittent renewable energy, thereby improving grid resiliency and reducing supply logistics. Herein, we report the feasibility of using molecular copper boron-imidazolate cages, BIF-29(Cu), to enable coupling between the electroreduction reaction of CO<sub>2</sub> (CO<sub>2</sub>RR) with NO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup> reduction (NO<sub>3</sub>RR) to produce urea with high selectivity of 68.5% and activity of 424 μA cm<sup>-2</sup>. Remarkably, BIF-29(Cu) is among the most selective systems for this multistep C-N coupling to-date, despite possessing isolated single-metal sites. The mechanism for C-N bond formation was probed with a combination of electrochemical analysis, in situ spectroscopy, and atomic-scale simulations. We found that NO<sub>3</sub>RR and CO<sub>2</sub>RR occur in tandem at separate copper sites with the most favorable C-N coupling pathway following the condensation between *CO and NH<sub>2</sub>OH to produce urea. This work highlights the utility of supramolecular metal-organic cages with atomically discrete active sites to enable highly efficient coupling reactions.
humanized_abstract: The use of electrocatalysis for the production of commodity chemicals has been demonstrated as an opportunity to leverage intermittent renewable energy for on-site and on-demand production, enhancing grid reliability and reducing complex logistics associated with supply chains. Here, we investigate the ability to couple CO2 reduction (CO2RR) with nitrate reduction (NO3RR) using a molecular copper boron-imidazolate cage material denoted as BIF-29(Cu). Urea production was achieved at an average selectivity of 68.5% and current density of 424 μA cm-2. We show that BIF-29(Cu) is one of the most selective materials reported for multistep C-N bond forming reactions, even though it only contains isolated single-metal sites. Insights into the C-N bond forming mechanism were obtained using a suite of techniques that included electrochemical measurements, in-situ vibrational spectroscopy and atomic-scale simulations. These studies indicated that NO3RR and CO2RR occurred at isolated copper sites, while the most favorable pathway for the C-N bond forming reaction occurs between *CO and *NH2OH to produce urea. Overall, our results suggest that the use of supramolecular metal-organic cages can provide a materials strategy to achieve efficient catalysts containing atomically discrete active sites.

These studies indicated that NO3RR and CO2RR occurred at isolated copper sites, while the most favorable pathway for the C-N bond forming reaction occurs between *CO and *NH2OH to produce urea.
